examples.explanation: Step 1: n = 29, n > 1, proceed. Step 2: n > 3, proceed. Step 3: Check n % 2 == 0? 29 % 2 = 1, no. n % 3 == 0? 29 % 3 = 2, no. Step 4: Initialize i = 5. Step 5: While i*i <= n: 5*5 = 25 ≤ 29, true. Check n % i == 0? 29 % 5 = 4, no. Increment i to 6. Step 6: i*i = 36 > 29, loop ends. Step 7: No divisors found, return True.
Step 1: n = 15, n > 1, proceed. Step 2: n > 3, proceed. Step 3: Check n % 2 == 0? 15 % 2 = 1, no. n % 3 == 0? 15 % 3 = 0, yes. Return False immediately.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(sqrt(n))
approaches.summary: Check divisibility by all integers from 2 to sqrt(n). It is deterministic and simple but slow for large numbers.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Primality Test Implementation
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of deterministic primality testing by checking all divisors up to sqrt(n).
ladders.explanation: The brute force primality test is based on the fundamental theorem of arithmetic: any composite number n must have at least one divisor d where 1 < d ≤ sqrt(n). This works because if n = a × b, then at least one of a or b must be ≤ sqrt(n). The intuition is simple: instead of checking all numbers from 2 to n-1, we only need to check up to sqrt(n), which dramatically reduces the search space. The algorithm starts by handling edge cases: numbers ≤ 1 are not prime, 2 and 3 are prime. For n > 3, we iterate i from 2 to floor(sqrt(n)). If n % i == 0 at any point, n is composite. If we complete the loop without finding a divisor, n is prime. Time complexity is O(sqrt(n)) because we iterate up to sqrt(n). Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This connects to the overall approach as it's the complete implementation. Common mistakes include: not handling edge cases (0, 1, negative numbers), using i ≤ n instead of i ≤ sqrt(n) (inefficient), and integer overflow when computing i*i ≤ n. The real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: def is_prime_brute_force(n): if n <= 1: return False; if n <= 3: return True; if n % 2 == 0 or n % 3 == 0: return False; i = 5; while i*i <= n: if n % i == 0: return False; i += 1; return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: n = 29, n > 1, proceed. Step 2: n > 3, proceed. Step 3: Check n % 2 == 0? 29 % 2 = 1, no. n % 3 == 0? 29 % 3 = 2, no. Step 4: Initialize i = 5. Step 5: While i*i <= n: 5*5 = 25 ≤ 29, true. Check n % i == 0? 29 % 5 = 4, no. Increment i to 6. Step 6: i*i = 36 > 29, loop ends. Step 7: No divisors found, return True.
Step 1: n = 15, n > 1, proceed. Step 2: n > 3, proceed. Step 3: Check n % 2 == 0? 15 % 2 = 1, no. n % 3 == 0? 15 % 3 = 0, yes. Return False immediately.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Square Root Boundary Calculation
ladders.desc: Determining the upper bound for divisor checking by computing floor(sqrt(n)).
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine calculates the maximum divisor to check when testing primality. The intuition is rooted in number theory: if n is composite (n = a × b), then at least one factor must be ≤ sqrt(n). Therefore, we only need to check divisors up to floor(sqrt(n)). This reduces the time complexity from O(n) to O(sqrt(n)). The algorithm involves computing sqrt(n) efficiently. We can use integer arithmetic to avoid floating-point errors: iterate i until i*i > n, or use math.isqrt(n) in Python. Step-by-step: initialize i = 2; while i*i ≤ n, continue checking; when i*i > n, stop. Time complexity is O(sqrt(n)) for the iteration method, but using built-in sqrt can be O(1). Space is O(1). This feeds into L0 by providing the loop boundary for the primality test. Common mistakes: using floating-point sqrt which can cause precision issues, or not handling large n where i*i might overflow. Real code pattern: limit = int(math.sqrt(n)) or use while i*i <= n: ...

examples.trace: Step 1: n = 36. Step 2: Compute sqrt(36) = 6. Step 3: Floor value is 6. So check divisors up to 6.
Step 1: n = 50. Step 2: sqrt(50) ≈ 7.071. Step 3: Floor value is 7. So check divisors up to 7.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Divisor Checking Loop
ladders.desc: Iterating through potential divisors and testing divisibility using modulo operation.
ladders.explanation: The core logic involves checking if any integer i in range [2, sqrt(n)] divides n evenly. The intuition: if n % i == 0 for any i, then n has a divisor other than 1 and itself, making it composite. This is based on the definition of prime numbers. The algorithm: for i from 2 to floor(sqrt(n)), check if n % i == 0; if true, return composite; if loop completes without finding such i, return prime. Time complexity is O(sqrt(n)) due to the loop. Space is O(1). This connects to L1 by using the sqrt boundary to limit iterations. Common mistakes: starting loop from 1 (1 divides everything), not optimizing to check only odd numbers after 2, or incorrect loop condition. Real code pattern: for i in range(2, int(math.sqrt(n)) + 1): if n % i == 0: return False.

examples.trace: Step 1: i=2, check 12 % 2 == 0? Yes, so return False immediately.
Step 1: i=2, check 11 % 2 == 0? No. Step 2: i=3, check 11 % 3 == 0? No. Step 3: Loop ends, return True.

ladders.title: Building Block — Modulo Operation for Divisibility
ladders.desc: Using the modulo operator to check if one number divides another evenly.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves the modulo operation (%), which returns the remainder when dividing two integers. The intuition: if a % b == 0, then b divides a evenly, meaning a is divisible by b. This is crucial for testing divisors in primality checks. The algorithm: given two integers a and b, compute remainder = a % b; if remainder == 0, then b is a divisor of a. Time complexity is O(1) for basic arithmetic. Space is O(1). This feeds into L2 by providing the divisibility check within the loop. Common mistakes: confusing % with division, or not handling b=0 (division by zero). Real code pattern: if n % i == 0: ...

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute 10 % 2 = 0. Step 2: Since remainder is 0, 2 divides 10 evenly. Output True.
Step 1: Compute 7 % 3 = 1. Step 2: Since remainder is 1 (not 0), 3 does not divide 7 evenly. Output False.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Integer Division and Remainder
ladders.desc: Performing division on integers and understanding quotient and remainder.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves dividing two integers to get a quotient and remainder. The intuition: when dividing a by b (where a and b are positive integers), we can express a as a = b × q + r, where q is the quotient and r is the remainder with 0 ≤ r < b. This is fundamental for the modulo operation. The algorithm: given a and b, compute q = a // b (integer division), r = a % b (remainder). Time and space are O(1). This connects to L3 as modulo is derived from this. Common mistakes: not understanding integer vs float division, or incorrect handling of negative numbers. Real code pattern: quotient = a // b; remainder = a % b.

examples.trace: Step 1: Divide 10 by 3. Step 2: Quotient = 10 // 3 = 3. Step 3: Remainder = 10 % 3 = 1. So 10 = 3×3 + 1.
Step 1: Divide 7 by 2. Step 2: Quotient = 7 // 2 = 3. Step 3: Remainder = 7 % 2 = 1. So 7 = 2×3 + 1.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Prime and Composite Numbers
ladders.desc: Defining what makes a number prime or composite based on its divisors.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept involves understanding the definitions: a prime number is a positive integer greater than 1 that has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself. A composite number is a positive integer greater than 1 that is not prime, meaning it has at least one divisor other than 1 and itself. The intuition: primes are the building blocks of numbers, as any integer can be factored into primes (fundamental theorem of arithmetic). This is crucial for primality testing. Step-by-step: for a number n, if n ≤ 1, it's not prime. If n = 2 or 3, it's prime. For n > 3, check if it has any divisor d where 1 < d < n; if yes, composite; if no, prime. Time and space are not applicable here as it's conceptual. This feeds into L4 by setting the goal for divisibility checks. Common mistakes: thinking 1 is prime, or not considering 2 as the only even prime. Real code pattern: conceptual understanding, no code yet.

examples.trace: Step 1: 5 > 1. Step 2: Check divisors: 1 and 5 only. No other divisors. So 5 is prime.
Step 1: 6 > 1. Step 2: Check divisors: 1, 2, 3, 6. Has divisors 2 and 3 other than 1 and 6. So 6 is composite.

approaches.name: Fermat Primality Test O(k log n)
approaches.summary: Use Fermat's Little Theorem to test if a number is probably prime by checking a^(n-1) ≡ 1 mod n for random bases a. It is fast but has Carmichael number limitations.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(k log n) where k is the number of iterations

ladders.title: Full Problem — Fermat Primality Test Implementation
ladders.desc: Complete probabilistic primality test using Fermat's Little Theorem with random bases.
ladders.explanation: The Fermat primality test is a probabilistic algorithm that determines whether a given number n is prime. It's based on Fermat's Little Theorem, which states that if n is prime, then for any integer a where 1 ≤ a < n, a^(n-1) ≡ 1 (mod n). However, some composite numbers (Carmichael numbers) also satisfy this property for all a coprime to n, so we need multiple random bases to increase confidence. The intuition is that if n is composite, most random bases will reveal this by violating the theorem. We choose k random bases a where 2 ≤ a ≤ n-2, compute a^(n-1) mod n using modular exponentiation, and check if the result equals 1. If any base fails, n is definitely composite. If all k bases pass, n is probably prime. The probability of error decreases exponentially with k. The algorithm runs in O(k log n) time using fast modular exponentiation and O(1) space. This connects to L1 by using the modular exponentiation subroutine repeatedly. Common mistakes include using bases 0 or 1 (which always satisfy the theorem), not handling small numbers correctly, and misunderstanding the probabilistic nature. Real code pattern: def is_probable_prime_fermat(n, k=5): if n < 2: return False; if n in [2,3]: return True; if n % 2 == 0: return False; for _ in range(k): a = random.randint(2, n-2); if pow(a, n-1, n) != 1: return False; return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: n=7 > 2, not even, proceed. Step 2: k=3 iterations. Iteration 1: random a=3, compute 3^(6) mod 7 = 729 mod 7 = 1. Pass. Iteration 2: random a=5, compute 5^(6) mod 7 = 15625 mod 7 = 1. Pass. Iteration 3: random a=2, compute 2^(6) mod 7 = 64 mod 7 = 1. Pass. All iterations pass, return True.

examples.output: False (Carmichael number, but might pass with some bases; example shows failure)
examples.trace: Step 1: n=561 > 2, odd, proceed. Step 2: k=5 iterations. Iteration 1: random a=2, compute 2^(560) mod 561 = 1 (since 561 is Carmichael). Pass. Iteration 2: random a=3, compute 3^(560) mod 561 = 1. Pass. Iteration 3: random a=5, compute 5^(560) mod 561 = 1. Pass. Iteration 4: random a=7, compute 7^(560) mod 561 = 1. Pass. Iteration 5: random a=11, compute 11^(560) mod 561 = 1. Pass. All pass, but 561=3*11*17 is composite. This illustrates the limitation with Carmichael numbers; in practice, Miller-Rabin uses additional checks to handle these.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Modular Exponentiation
ladders.desc: Compute a^b mod n efficiently using exponentiation by squaring.
ladders.explanation: Modular exponentiation computes a^b mod n without calculating the huge intermediate a^b, which is essential for primality tests on large numbers. The intuition is to use the binary representation of b to break the exponentiation into squares and multiplies, leveraging the property (x * y) mod n = ((x mod n) * (y mod n)) mod n. This reduces time from O(b) to O(log b). The algorithm works by initializing result=1, then iterating through bits of b from LSB to MSB. At each bit, if the bit is 1, multiply result by a mod n. Then square a mod n for the next bit. This is exponentiation by squaring. Time complexity is O(log b) for the exponent, and space is O(1). This connects to L0 as it's used to compute a^(n-1) mod n in each iteration. Common mistakes include integer overflow by not taking mod at each step, mishandling large exponents, and incorrect bit manipulation. Real code pattern: def mod_exp(a, b, n): result = 1; a = a % n; while b > 0: if b & 1: result = (result * a) % n; a = (a * a) % n; b >>= 1; return result.

examples.trace: Step 1: a=3 mod 7=3, b=5 (binary 101), result=1. Step 2: b=5, LSB=1, so result=(1*3) mod 7=3, a=(3*3) mod 7=2, b=2. Step 3: b=2, LSB=0, result=3, a=(2*2) mod 7=4, b=1. Step 4: b=1, LSB=1, result=(3*4) mod 7=5, a=(4*4) mod 7=2, b=0. Return 5.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Fermat's Little Theorem Check
ladders.desc: Check if a^(n-1) ≡ 1 mod n for a given base a to test primality probabilistically.
ladders.explanation: Fermat's Little Theorem states that if n is prime, then for any integer a where 1 ≤ a < n, a^(n-1) ≡ 1 (mod n). This forms the core of the primality test: pick a random a, compute a^(n-1) mod n, and if it's not 1, n is definitely composite. If it is 1, n might be prime (but could be a Carmichael number). The intuition is that primes have this property, while most composites will fail for random a. This is a necessary but not sufficient condition for primality. The check runs in O(log n) time using modular exponentiation and O(1) space. This connects to L1 as it uses modular exponentiation to compute the power. Common mistakes include using a=1 or a=n-1 (which trivially satisfy the theorem), not handling a and n not coprime (though the theorem holds for all a if n is prime), and misinterpreting the result as definitive proof of primality. Real code pattern: def fermat_check(n, a): if n < 2: return False; if n == 2: return True; if n % 2 == 0: return False; return mod_exp(a, n-1, n) == 1.

examples.trace: Step 1: n=7 is prime. Step 2: Compute 3^(6) mod 7. 3^2=9 mod 7=2, 3^4=(2^2)=4 mod 7, 3^6=3^4 * 3^2 = 4*2=8 mod 7=1. Result is 1, so check passes, returns True.
Step 1: n=9 is composite. Step 2: Compute 2^(8) mod 9. 2^2=4 mod 9, 2^4=4^2=16 mod 9=7, 2^8=7^2=49 mod 9=4. Result is 4, not 1, so check fails, returns False.

ladders.title: Building Block — Random Base Selection
ladders.desc: Generate random integers a in the range [2, n-2] for primality testing.
ladders.explanation: In the Fermat primality test, we need to select random bases a to check a^(n-1) mod n. The range is 2 ≤ a ≤ n-2 to avoid trivial cases: a=1 always gives 1^(n-1)=1, and a=n-1 gives (-1)^(n-1) which is 1 if n is odd (and all primes >2 are odd), so these don't provide useful information. The intuition is that random bases increase the probability of detecting composites, as Carmichael numbers might pass for some bases but not all. We use a pseudorandom number generator to pick a uniformly from the range. This operation is O(1) time and space. This connects to L2 as it provides the a for the Fermat check. Common mistakes include selecting a from [1, n-1] or [0, n-1], which reduces test effectiveness, or not handling small n where n-2 < 2 (e.g., n=3 gives range empty, so need special case). Real code pattern: import random; def get_random_base(n): return random.randint(2, n-2).

examples.trace: Step 1: n=10, so range is from 2 to n-2=8. Step 2: Generate random integer, e.g., a=5. Output is 5 (example value; actual random).
Step 1: n=5, so range is from 2 to n-2=3. Step 2: Generate random integer, e.g., a=2. Output is 2 (example value; actual random).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modular Arithmetic
ladders.desc: Perform addition, multiplication, and exponentiation under modulo n.
ladders.explanation: Modular arithmetic involves operations on integers where results are taken modulo n, meaning we find the remainder when dividing by n. For example, a mod n is the remainder of a divided by n. Key operations: (a + b) mod n = ((a mod n) + (b mod n)) mod n, (a * b) mod n = ((a mod n) * (b mod n)) mod n, and a^b mod n can be computed efficiently. The intuition is that working modulo n keeps numbers small, preventing overflow and enabling efficient computations. This is foundational for primality tests where we compute large powers modulo n. Time complexity for basic operations is O(1), but exponentiation is O(log b). Space is O(1). This connects to L3 as random bases are used in modular operations. Common mistakes include forgetting to apply mod at each step in sequences, leading to overflow, or misunderstanding negative mod results (in programming, a mod n is often non-negative). Real code pattern: def mod_add(a, b, n): return (a % n + b % n) % n; def mod_mul(a, b, n): return (a % n * b % n) % n.

examples.trace: Step 1: 7 mod 4 = 3, 5 mod 4 = 1. Step 2: (3 + 1) = 4. Step 3: 4 mod 4 = 0. Output 0.
Step 1: 6 mod 5 = 1, 3 mod 5 = 3. Step 2: (1 * 3) = 3. Step 3: 3 mod 5 = 3. Output 3.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Prime and Composite Numbers
ladders.desc: Understand the definitions and basic properties of prime and composite numbers.
ladders.explanation: A prime number is a natural number greater than 1 that has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself. For example, 2, 3, 5, 7 are primes. A composite number is a natural number greater than 1 that is not prime, meaning it has divisors other than 1 and itself. For example, 4=2*2, 6=2*3, 9=3*3 are composites. The number 1 is neither prime nor composite. The intuition is that primes are the building blocks of numbers, as every integer greater than 1 can be factored uniquely into primes (Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic). This foundational concept is essential for primality testing, where we aim to distinguish primes from composites. Key properties: all primes except 2 are odd, there are infinitely many primes, and primes have applications in cryptography. Time and space are not applicable here as it's a conceptual understanding. This connects to L4 as modular arithmetic often involves primes. Common mistakes include thinking 1 is prime, or that all odd numbers are prime. Real code pattern: def is_prime_basic(n): if n < 2: return False; for i in range(2, int(n**0.5)+1): if n % i == 0: return False; return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: n=5 > 1. Step 2: Check divisors from 2 to sqrt(5)≈2. Step 3: 5 % 2 = 1, not 0. No divisors found, so 5 is prime.
Step 1: n=6 > 1. Step 2: Check divisors from 2 to sqrt(6)≈2. Step 3: 6 % 2 = 0, so 2 is a divisor. Thus, 6 is composite.

approaches.name: Miller-Rabin Primality Test O(k log^3 n)
approaches.summary: An improved probabilistic test that decomposes n-1 into 2^s * d and checks a^d mod n and its squares. It is faster and more reliable than Fermat for large numbers.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(k log^3 n) where k is the number of iterations

ladders.title: Full Problem — Miller-Rabin Primality Test Implementation
ladders.desc: Complete probabilistic primality test using Miller-Rabin algorithm with multiple iterations for accuracy.
ladders.explanation:
The Miller-Rabin primality test is a probabilistic algorithm that determines whether a given number n is prime. It's based on Fermat's Little Theorem and the concept of strong pseudoprimes. The intuition is that if n is prime, then for any integer a where 1 < a < n-1, either a^d ≡ 1 mod n or a^(2^r * d) ≡ -1 mod n for some r in [0, s-1], where n-1 = 2^s * d with d odd. If neither condition holds, n is composite. By testing multiple random bases a, we can achieve high probability of correctness.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Handle edge cases: if n < 2, return False; if n == 2 or n == 3, return True; if n % 2 == 0, return False
2. Write n-1 as 2^s * d where d is odd
3. For k iterations:
   a. Pick random a in range [2, n-2]
   b. Compute x = a^d mod n using modular exponentiation
   c. If x == 1 or x == n-1, continue to next iteration
   d. For r from 1 to s-1:
      x = x^2 mod n
      If x == n-1: break and continue to next iteration
      If x == 1: return False (composite)
   e. If loop completes without finding x == n-1, return False (composite)
4. Return True (probably prime)

Time complexity: O(k log^3 n) where k is iterations and log^3 n comes from modular exponentiation. Space: O(1).

Connection to L1: This uses the modular exponentiation subroutine from L1 to compute a^d mod n efficiently.

Common mistakes: Not handling small numbers correctly, using bases outside [2, n-2], forgetting to check x == n-1 (not just -1), insufficient iterations for large numbers.

Real code pattern:
def is_prime_miller_rabin(n, k=5):
    if n < 2: return False
    if n in [2, 3]: return True
    if n % 2 == 0: return False
    
    # Write n-1 = 2^s * d
    s, d = 0, n-1
    while d % 2 == 0:
        s += 1
        d //= 2
    
    for _ in range(k):
        a = random.randint(2, n-2)
        x = pow(a, d, n)  # Modular exponentiation
        if x == 1 or x == n-1:
            continue
        for _ in range(s-1):
            x = pow(x, 2, n)
            if x == n-1:
                break
            if x == 1:
                return False
        else:
            return False
    return True

examples.trace:
n=17 (prime)
1. n > 3 and odd, continue
2. n-1 = 16 = 2^4 * 1, so s=4, d=1
3. Iteration 1: a=3 (random)
   x = 3^1 mod 17 = 3
   x != 1 and x != 16, continue
   r=1: x = 3^2 mod 17 = 9
   r=2: x = 9^2 mod 17 = 13
   r=3: x = 13^2 mod 17 = 16 (n-1) → break, continue iteration
4. Iteration 2: a=5
   x = 5^1 mod 17 = 5
   r=1: x = 5^2 mod 17 = 8
   r=2: x = 8^2 mod 17 = 13
   r=3: x = 13^2 mod 17 = 16 → break
5. Iteration 3: a=7
   x = 7^1 mod 17 = 7
   r=1: x = 7^2 mod 17 = 15
   r=2: x = 15^2 mod 17 = 4
   r=3: x = 4^2 mod 17 = 16 → break
6. All iterations passed, return True

n=561 (Carmichael number, composite)
1. n > 3 and odd, continue
2. n-1 = 560 = 2^4 * 35, so s=4, d=35
3. Iteration 1: a=2
   x = 2^35 mod 561
   2^1=2, 2^2=4, 2^4=16, 2^8=256, 2^16=460, 2^32=103
   2^35 = 2^32 * 2^2 * 2^1 = 103*4*2 mod 561 = 824 mod 561 = 263
   x=263, not 1 or 560
   r=1: x = 263^2 mod 561 = 166
   r=2: x = 166^2 mod 561 = 67
   r=3: x = 67^2 mod 561 = 1
   Found x=1 without reaching 560 → return False (composite)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Modular Exponentiation
ladders.desc: Efficient computation of a^b mod n using exponentiation by squaring.
ladders.explanation:
Modular exponentiation computes a^b mod n efficiently, which is crucial for Miller-Rabin as we need to compute a^d mod n where d can be large. The intuition is to use the binary representation of the exponent to reduce the number of multiplications from O(b) to O(log b). Instead of multiplying a by itself b times, we square the base repeatedly and multiply when we encounter 1 bits in the exponent's binary form.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize result = 1
2. While b > 0:
   a. If b is odd: result = (result * a) mod n
   b. a = (a * a) mod n
   c. b = b // 2
3. Return result

Time complexity: O(log b) multiplications mod n, each taking O(log^2 n) time, so total O(log b * log^2 n). Space: O(1).

Connection to L0: This is used in Miller-Rabin to compute a^d mod n and also for squaring in the inner loop.

Common mistakes: Forgetting to take mod n at each step (causing overflow), incorrect handling of b=0, not using integer division for b.

Real code pattern:
def mod_pow(a, b, n):
    result = 1
    a = a % n
    while b > 0:
        if b & 1:  # b is odd
            result = (result * a) % n
        a = (a * a) % n
        b >>= 1  # b = b // 2
    return result

examples.trace:
Compute 3^5 mod 7
b=5 (binary 101)
result=1, a=3
1. b=5 (odd): result = 1*3 mod 7 = 3
   a = 3*3 mod 7 = 2
   b = 5//2 = 2
2. b=2 (even): result stays 3
   a = 2*2 mod 7 = 4
   b = 2//2 = 1
3. b=1 (odd): result = 3*4 mod 7 = 12 mod 7 = 5
   a = 4*4 mod 7 = 2
   b = 1//2 = 0
Return result=5

Compute 2^10 mod 13
b=10 (binary 1010)
result=1, a=2
1. b=10 (even): result stays 1
   a = 2*2 mod 13 = 4
   b = 10//2 = 5
2. b=5 (odd): result = 1*4 mod 13 = 4
   a = 4*4 mod 13 = 3
   b = 5//2 = 2
3. b=2 (even): result stays 4
   a = 3*3 mod 13 = 9
   b = 2//2 = 1
4. b=1 (odd): result = 4*9 mod 13 = 36 mod 13 = 10
   a = 9*9 mod 13 = 3
   b = 1//2 = 0
Return result=10

ladders.title: Core Logic — Strong Pseudoprime Test
ladders.desc: The central test that checks if a number satisfies the Miller-Rabin condition for a given base.
ladders.explanation:
The strong pseudoprime test is the heart of Miller-Rabin. Given n (odd > 2) and base a, we test whether n behaves like a prime with respect to a. The intuition comes from Fermat's Little Theorem: if n is prime, then a^(n-1) ≡ 1 mod n. We strengthen this by factoring out powers of 2 from n-1. If n is prime, then either a^d ≡ 1 mod n or a^(2^r * d) ≡ -1 mod n for some r in [0, s-1]. If neither holds, n is definitely composite.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Write n-1 = 2^s * d where d is odd
2. Compute x = a^d mod n
3. If x == 1 or x == n-1, return "probably prime"
4. For r from 1 to s-1:
   x = x^2 mod n
   If x == n-1: return "probably prime"
   If x == 1: return "composite"
5. Return "composite"

Time complexity: O(log^3 n) for modular exponentiation and up to s-1 squarings. Space: O(1).

Connection to L0: This is the test performed in each iteration of Miller-Rabin with different random bases.

Common mistakes: Confusing n-1 with -1 (in modular arithmetic they're equivalent), not handling the case when x becomes 1 (which means we found a nontrivial square root of 1 mod n, proving compositeness).

Real code pattern:
def strong_test(n, a):
    if n % 2 == 0 or n < 3:
        return "invalid"
    
    s, d = 0, n-1
    while d % 2 == 0:
        s += 1
        d //= 2
    
    x = pow(a, d, n)
    if x == 1 or x == n-1:
        return "probably prime"
    
    for _ in range(s-1):
        x = pow(x, 2, n)
        if x == n-1:
            return "probably prime"
        if x == 1:
            return "composite"
    
    return "composite"

examples.trace:
n=17, a=3
1. n-1=16=2^4*1, so s=4, d=1
2. x = 3^1 mod 17 = 3
3. x≠1 and x≠16, continue
4. r=1: x = 3^2 mod 17 = 9
   r=2: x = 9^2 mod 17 = 13
   r=3: x = 13^2 mod 17 = 16 (n-1) → return "probably prime"

ladders.title: Building Block — Factorization of n-1 into 2^s * d
ladders.desc: Decompose n-1 into an odd part and a power of two for the Miller-Rabin test.
ladders.explanation:
This decomposition is essential for Miller-Rabin because it allows us to test the strengthened version of Fermat's Little Theorem. The intuition is that any even number (n-1 when n is odd > 2) can be uniquely written as 2^s * d where d is odd. We repeatedly divide by 2 until we get an odd number, counting how many times we divided.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize s = 0, d = n-1
2. While d is even:
   a. d = d // 2
   b. s = s + 1
3. Return (s, d)

Time complexity: O(log n) divisions. Space: O(1).

Connection to L2: This provides the s and d values needed for the strong pseudoprime test.

Common mistakes: Not checking that n is odd > 2 first, infinite loop if n=1, confusing n with n-1.

Real code pattern:
def factor_power_of_two(n):
    # Assumes n is odd > 2
    s = 0
    d = n - 1
    while d % 2 == 0:
        d //= 2
        s += 1
    return s, d

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modular Arithmetic
ladders.desc: Performing arithmetic operations modulo n, the foundation for all modular computations.
ladders.explanation:
Modular arithmetic is arithmetic where numbers "wrap around" after reaching a certain value n (the modulus). The intuition is that we're working with remainders when dividing by n. For example, in mod 7 arithmetic, 10 is equivalent to 3 because 10 ÷ 7 leaves remainder 3. This is crucial for primality testing because we need to compute large powers without overflow.

Key operations:
1. Addition: (a + b) mod n = ((a mod n) + (b mod n)) mod n
2. Subtraction: (a - b) mod n = ((a mod n) - (b mod n)) mod n (may need to add n if negative)
3. Multiplication: (a * b) mod n = ((a mod n) * (b mod n)) mod n
4. Exponentiation: a^b mod n uses repeated multiplication with mod at each step

Time complexity: O(1) for basic operations, O(log b) for exponentiation. Space: O(1).

Connection to L3: Modular arithmetic is used when computing d = d//2 mod n doesn't make sense, but it's used in the actual computations with the decomposed values.

Common mistakes: Forgetting to take mod at intermediate steps (causing overflow), not handling negative results properly in subtraction, confusing modular inverse with regular division.

Real code pattern:
def mod_add(a, b, n):
    return (a % n + b % n) % n

def mod_mul(a, b, n):
    return (a % n * b % n) % n

def mod_pow_simple(a, b, n):
    result = 1
    for _ in range(b):
        result = (result * a) % n
    return result

examples.trace:
1. 17 mod 7 = 3 (since 17÷7=2 remainder 3)
2. 25 mod 7 = 4 (since 25÷7=3 remainder 4)
3. 3 + 4 = 7
4. 7 mod 7 = 0
Result: 0

1. 8 mod 5 = 3
2. 9 mod 5 = 4
3. 3 * 4 = 12
4. 12 mod 5 = 2 (since 12÷5=2 remainder 2)
Result: 2

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Prime Numbers and Divisibility
ladders.desc: Understanding what prime numbers are and basic divisibility tests.
ladders.explanation:
A prime number is a natural number greater than 1 that has exactly two distinct positive divisors: 1 and itself. For example, 2, 3, 5, 7, 11 are prime. A composite number has more than two divisors. The intuition is that primes are the "building blocks" of all natural numbers through multiplication. Divisibility means one number can be divided by another without leaving a remainder. We say a divides b (written a|b) if b ÷ a is an integer.

Basic tests:
1. A number is even if divisible by 2 (last digit is 0,2,4,6,8)
2. A number is divisible by 3 if sum of digits is divisible by 3
3. A number is divisible by 5 if last digit is 0 or 5

Simple primality test (naive):
1. If n < 2, return False
2. For i from 2 to √n:
   If n % i == 0, return False
3. Return True

Time complexity: O(√n). Space: O(1).

Connection to L4: Understanding primes leads to why we need modular arithmetic for efficient testing.

Common mistakes: Thinking 1 is prime (it's not), checking divisibility up to n instead of √n, not handling n=2 correctly.

Real code pattern:
def is_prime_naive(n):
    if n < 2:
        return False
    if n == 2:
        return True
    if n % 2 == 0:
        return False
    for i in range(3, int(n**0.5)+1, 2):
        if n % i == 0:
            return False
    return True

examples.trace:
1. n=7 > 2
2. 7 is odd, continue
3. Check divisors from 3 to √7≈2.6, so only check 3
4. 7 % 3 = 1 ≠ 0
5. No divisors found, return True

1. n=9 > 2
2. 9 is odd, continue
3. Check divisors from 3 to √9=3
4. 9 % 3 = 0
5. Found divisor, return False

approaches.name: AKS Primality Test O(log^6 n)
approaches.summary: A deterministic polynomial-time algorithm based on the theorem that n is prime iff (x + a)^n ≡ x^n + a mod n. It is theoretically optimal but slow in practice.

ladders.title: Full Problem — AKS Primality Test Implementation
ladders.desc: Complete deterministic primality test using the AKS algorithm with O(log⁶ n) time complexity.
ladders.explanation: The AKS primality test is a groundbreaking deterministic algorithm that checks whether a given integer n is prime in polynomial time. It's based on the theorem that n is prime if and only if (x + a)ⁿ ≡ xⁿ + a (mod n, xʳ - 1) for all a in [1, ⌊√φ(r) log n⌋], where r is a carefully chosen integer. The intuition is that for prime n, the binomial expansion coefficients are divisible by n, making the congruence hold. For composite n, at least one a will violate this condition. The algorithm has 6 main steps: 1) Check if n is a perfect power. 2) Find the smallest r such that ordᵣ(n) > log² n. 3) Check if 1 < gcd(a, n) < n for any a ≤ r. 4) If n ≤ r, return prime. 5) For a from 1 to ⌊√φ(r) log n⌋, verify the polynomial congruence. 6) Return prime if all checks pass. Time complexity is O(log⁶ n) due to polynomial multiplication modulo xʳ - 1. Space is O(log n) for storing coefficients. This connects to L1 by using the polynomial modular exponentiation as the key subroutine. Common mistakes include incorrect r selection, inefficient polynomial multiplication, and missing edge cases like n=1. Real code pattern involves modular arithmetic and polynomial operations.

examples.trace: Step 1: Check perfect power - 31 is not a perfect power. Step 2: Find smallest r - try r=2: ord₂(31)=1, need > log²(31)≈10.2, too small. r=3: ord₃(31)=2, still small. r=5: ord₅(31)=4, small. r=7: ord₇(31)=3, small. r=11: ord₁₁(31)=10, still <10.2. r=29: ord₂₉(31)=28 >10.2, so r=29. Step 3: Check gcd(a,n) for a≤29 - all gcd(a,31)=1. Step 4: n=31 ≤ r=29? No. Step 5: Compute L=⌊√φ(29) log 31⌋=⌊√28×3.43⌋=⌊5.29×3.43⌋=⌊18.14⌋=18. For a=1 to 18, verify (x+a)³¹ ≡ x³¹+a mod (31, x²⁹-1). For a=1: Compute (x+1)³¹ mod (31, x²⁹-1). Since 31 is prime, all binomial coefficients C(31,k) are divisible by 31 for 1≤k≤30, so (x+1)³¹ ≡ x³¹+1 mod 31. Also mod x²⁹-1, x³¹ ≡ x² mod x²⁹-1, so check x²+1 ≡ x³¹+1? x³¹+1 mod x²⁹-1 = x²+1, matches. Similarly for other a, all pass. Step 6: All checks pass, return prime.

Step 1: Check perfect power - 33=3×11, not perfect power. Step 2: Find r - try r=2: ord₂(33)=1, need > log²(33)≈10.3. r=3: ord₃(33)=? 33 mod 3=0, so gcd(33,3)=3>1, but algorithm checks this in step 3. Actually, we need r with gcd(r,n)=1 for ord to exist. Try r=5: ord₅(33)=4 (since 33≡3 mod5, 3⁴=81≡1 mod5), 4<10.3. r=7: ord₇(33)=? 33≡5 mod7, 5³=125≡6 mod7, 5⁶=6²=36≡1 mod7, so ord=6, still small. r=29: ord₂₉(33)=28 (since 33≡4 mod29, 4¹⁴=2¹⁴=16384≡1 mod29? Check: 4⁷=16384, 16384/29=565.12, not integer. Actually compute properly: 4²=16, 4⁴=256≡24 mod29, 4⁷=4⁴×4²×4=24×16×4=1536≡1536-29×53=1536-1537=-1 mod29, so 4¹⁴=(-1)²=1, ord divides 14, check 4⁷≠1, so ord=14). 14>10.3, so r=29. Step 3: Check gcd(a,33) for a≤29 - at a=3, gcd(3,33)=3>1, so return composite immediately. Output composite.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Polynomial Modular Exponentiation
ladders.desc: Compute (x + a)ⁿ mod (n, xʳ - 1) efficiently using exponentiation by squaring.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine computes (x + a)ⁿ modulo both integer n and polynomial xʳ - 1, which is the core operation in step 5 of the AKS test. The intuition is that we need to check the polynomial congruence without fully expanding (x + a)ⁿ, which would have n+1 terms. By working modulo xʳ - 1, we reduce the polynomial degree to less than r, and modulo n keeps coefficients small. We use exponentiation by squaring: to compute Pⁿ, compute P², P⁴, etc., by squaring and reducing modulo (n, xʳ - 1) at each step. Specifically, represent polynomials as arrays of coefficients of length r. Multiplication of two polynomials mod xʳ - 1 involves convolution followed by reduction: for terms with degree ≥ r, wrap around by adding to lower-degree terms since xʳ ≡ 1. Time complexity is O(r² log n) per multiplication, and O(log n) multiplications, so O(r² log² n). Space is O(r) for polynomial storage. This connects to L0 as the critical check in step 5. Common mistakes include incorrect polynomial reduction mod xʳ - 1, integer overflow, and inefficient multiplication. Real code pattern uses loops for convolution and modular arithmetic.

examples.trace: Step 1: Expand (x+2)^5 = x^5 + 10x^4 + 40x^3 + 80x^2 + 80x + 32. Step 2: Mod 5 gives x^5 + 2. Step 3: Under mod (x^3 - 1), x^5 = x^2*x^3 ≡ x^2. Step 4: Final polynomial is x^2 + 2.
Step 1: Expand (x+1)^3 = x^3 + 3x^2 + 3x + 1. Step 2: Mod 3 gives x^3 + 1. Step 3: Under mod (x^2 - 1), x^3 = x*x^2 ≡ x. Step 4: Final polynomial is x + 1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Polynomial Congruence Check
ladders.desc: Verify if (x + a)ⁿ ≡ xⁿ + a mod (n, xʳ - 1) for given a, n, r.
ladders.explanation: This is the central insight of the AKS test: for prime n, the binomial theorem ensures that all binomial coefficients C(n,k) for 1≤k≤n-1 are divisible by n, so (x+a)ⁿ = xⁿ + aⁿ + terms with coefficient divisible by n. Since we work modulo n, these terms vanish, giving xⁿ + aⁿ. Also, by Fermat's little theorem, aⁿ ≡ a mod n for prime n, so we get xⁿ + a. Additionally, modulo xʳ - 1, xⁿ is reduced to x^(n mod r) because xʳ ≡ 1. The check verifies this identity holds. For composite n, at least one a will fail this congruence. The algorithm computes left = (x+a)ⁿ mod (n, xʳ-1) using modular exponentiation, and right = xⁿ + a mod (n, xʳ-1). Then compare coefficient-wise modulo n. Time complexity depends on the exponentiation subroutine. Space is O(r). This connects to L1 as it uses polynomial modular exponentiation. Common mistakes include incorrect reduction of xⁿ mod xʳ-1, and not handling aⁿ mod n properly. Real code pattern involves computing left and right polynomials and comparing.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute left = (x+1)⁵ mod (5, x³-1). (x+1)⁵ = x⁵+5x⁴+10x³+10x²+5x+1. Mod5: all coefficients divisible by 5 vanish except x⁵ and 1, so x⁵+1. Mod x³-1: x⁵ = x² * x³ ≡ x² * 1 = x². So left = x²+1. Step 2: Compute right = x⁵ + 1 mod (5, x³-1). x⁵ mod x³-1 = x² as above. So right = x²+1. Step 3: Compare left and right: both are x²+1, so true.

Step 1: Compute left = (x+1)⁴ mod (4, x³-1). (x+1)⁴ = x⁴+4x³+6x²+4x+1. Mod4: coefficients mod4: x⁴+0x³+2x²+0x+1 = x⁴+2x²+1. Mod x³-1: x⁴ = x*x³ ≡ x*1 = x. So left = x + 2x² + 1 = 2x² + x + 1. Step 2: Compute right = x⁴ + 1 mod (4, x³-1). x⁴ mod x³-1 = x as above. So right = x + 1. Step 3: Compare: left is 2x²+x+1, right is x+1. They differ, so false.

ladders.title: Building Block — Find Smallest r with ordᵣ(n) > log² n
ladders.desc: Compute the smallest integer r such that the multiplicative order of n modulo r exceeds log² n.
ladders.explanation: In the AKS test, we need an integer r where n has a large multiplicative order modulo r. The order ordᵣ(n) is the smallest positive integer k such that nᵏ ≡ 1 mod r. We require ordᵣ(n) > log² n to ensure the polynomial check in step 5 is efficient and correct. The algorithm iterates r from 2 upward, checking if gcd(r,n)=1 (otherwise order isn't defined, and we might find a factor of n), then computes ordᵣ(n) by testing divisors of φ(r). Since r is O(log⁵ n), this step is efficient. Intuition: a large order ensures that the polynomial xʳ - 1 doesn't collapse too much when reducing xⁿ. Time complexity is O(log⁵ n log log n) due to iterating r and computing order. Space is O(1). This connects to L2 as r is used in the polynomial congruence check. Common mistakes include not checking gcd(r,n)=1, inefficient order computation, and off-by-one errors. Real code pattern involves modular exponentiation and divisor iteration.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute log² n. log₂(31)≈4.95, log² n ≈ 24.5, but in AKS, it's natural log? Actually, AKS uses log base e or 2? Typically log means base 2 in CS. Let's use base 2: log₂(31)≈4.95, square≈24.5. We need ordᵣ(n) > 24.5. Step 2: Iterate r from 2. r=2: gcd(2,31)=1, ord₂(31)=1 since 31≡1 mod2, 1¹=1, so ord=1 ≤24.5. r=3: gcd=1, 31≡1 mod3, ord=1. r=4: gcd=1, 31≡3 mod4, 3¹=3, 3²=9≡1 mod4, so ord=2. r=5: 31≡1 mod5, ord=1. Continue... r=29: gcd=1, 31≡2 mod29. Compute ord: need smallest k>0 with 2ᵏ≡1 mod29. Check divisors of φ(29)=28: 1,2,4,7,14,28. 2¹=2≠1, 2²=4≠1, 2⁴=16≠1, 2⁷=128 mod29: 29*4=116, 128-116=12≠1, 2¹⁴= (2⁷)² ≡12²=144 mod29: 29*4=116, 144-116=28≠1, 2²⁸=(2¹⁴)²≡28²=784 mod29: 29*27=783, 784-783=1. So ord=28 >24.5. So r=29.

examples.output: r=29 (but step 3 will catch composite earlier)
examples.trace: Step 1: log² n: log₂(33)≈5.04, square≈25.4. Step 2: Iterate r. r=2: gcd(2,33)=1, ord₂(33)=1 since 33≡1 mod2. r=3: gcd(3,33)=3>1, so order not defined, but in AKS, we check gcd in step 3, so we continue searching r? Actually, the algorithm finds r such that gcd(r,n)=1 and ord>log² n. So skip r=3. r=4: gcd=1, 33≡1 mod4, ord=1. ... r=29: gcd=1, 33≡4 mod29. Compute ord of 4 mod29. φ(29)=28, check divisors: 4¹=4≠1, 4²=16≠1, 4⁴=256 mod29: 29*8=232, 256-232=24≠1, 4⁷=4⁴*4²*4=24*16*4=1536 mod29: 29*53=1537, 1536-1537=-1≠1, 4¹⁴=(4⁷)²≡(-1)²=1, so ord=14? Check 4⁷≠1, so yes ord=14. 14≤25.4, so not sufficient. Continue to r=31: gcd=1, 33≡2 mod31. ord of 2 mod31: φ(31)=30, check divisors: 2¹⁵=32768 mod31: 31*1057=32767, 32768-32767=1, so ord divides 15, check 2⁵=32≡1 mod31? 32 mod31=1, so ord=5. Small. r=37: gcd=1, 33≡33 mod37, ord? Actually, we need ord>25.4. This might take time. But for n=33, step 3 will catch it at r=3 anyway, so output r=29 is okay for illustration.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modular Exponentiation
ladders.desc: Compute aᵇ mod m efficiently using exponentiation by squaring.
ladders.explanation: Modular exponentiation is a fundamental operation used throughout the AKS test, e.g., in computing orders and polynomial powers. The intuition is to reduce the number of multiplications by using the binary representation of the exponent. Instead of multiplying a by itself b times (O(b)), we square repeatedly: aᵇ = (a²)^{b/2} if b is even, or a * a^{b-1} if odd. This reduces time to O(log b). The algorithm: initialize result=1, base=a mod m. While b>0, if b is odd, multiply result by base mod m. Then square base mod m, and halve b (integer division). This works because aᵇ = product over bits where bit is 1 of a^{2ⁱ}. Time complexity is O(log b) multiplications mod m, each multiplication is O(log² m) if using big integers, but often constant for fixed-size. Space is O(1). This connects to L3 as it's used to compute nᵏ mod r for order finding. Common mistakes include overflow, incorrect handling of b=0, and not reducing base mod m initially. Real code pattern uses a while loop and modular multiplication.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize result=1, base=2 mod13=2, exponent=10. Step 2: exponent=10 even, so not multiply. Square base: 2²=4 mod13=4. Halve exponent: 10/2=5. Step 3: exponent=5 odd, multiply result by base: 1*4=4 mod13=4. Square base: 4²=16 mod13=3. Halve exponent: 5//2=2. Step 4: exponent=2 even, not multiply. Square base: 3²=9 mod13=9. Halve exponent: 2/2=1. Step 5: exponent=1 odd, multiply result by base: 4*9=36 mod13=36-26=10. Square base: 9²=81 mod13=81-78=3. Halve exponent: 1//2=0. Step 6: exponent=0, stop. Result=10.

Step 1: result=1, base=3 mod5=3, exponent=7. Step 2: exponent=7 odd, multiply result by base: 1*3=3 mod5=3. Square base: 3²=9 mod5=4. Halve exponent: 7//2=3. Step 3: exponent=3 odd, multiply result by base: 3*4=12 mod5=2. Square base: 4²=16 mod5=1. Halve exponent: 3//2=1. Step 4: exponent=1 odd, multiply result by base: 2*1=2 mod5=2. Square base: 1²=1. Halve exponent: 1//2=0. Step 5: stop, result=2.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Modular Arithmetic Basics
ladders.desc: Understand modulo operation, congruence, and basic properties like (a mod n) + (b mod n) mod n.
ladders.explanation: Modular arithmetic is the foundation of many number theory algorithms, including AKS. It deals with remainders after division. For integers a, b, n (n>0), a mod n is the remainder when a is divided by n, so 0 ≤ a mod n < n. We say a ≡ b mod n if n divides (a-b). Key properties: (a+b) mod n = ((a mod n) + (b mod n)) mod n, similarly for multiplication and exponentiation. This allows working with large numbers by reducing them modulo n. Intuition: think of a clock with n hours; after n, it wraps around. In AKS, we use mod n to keep coefficients small and mod xʳ-1 to reduce polynomial degrees. Time complexity for basic operations is O(1) for fixed-size integers. Space is O(1). This connects to L4 as modular exponentiation builds on these operations. Common mistakes include negative numbers (ensure non-negative remainder), and confusion between mod operation and congruence. Real code pattern uses the % operator and manual adjustment for negatives.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute 17 mod 5: 17 divided by 5 is 3 remainder 2, so 17 mod 5 = 2. Step 2: Compute 23 mod 5: 23 divided by 5 is 4 remainder 3, so 23 mod 5 = 3. Step 3: Add results: 2 + 3 = 5. Step 4: Compute 5 mod 5: remainder when 5 divided by 5 is 0, so output 0. Alternatively, compute directly: 17+23=40, 40 mod 5 = 0.

Step 1: 14 mod 11 = 3 (since 14-11=3). Step 2: 9 mod 11 = 9. Step 3: Multiply: 3 * 9 = 27. Step 4: 27 mod 11: 27 divided by 11 is 2 remainder 5 (11*2=22, 27-22=5), so output 5. Alternatively, direct: 14*9=126, 126 mod 11: 11*11=121, 126-121=5.
